title: Sales of Valve's Steam Deck crater after recent price hike
gist: Steam Deck sales appear to have fallen roughly 80 percent from 2025 levels since Valve relaunched the handheld at a much higher $789-$949 price in May, driven by RAM/storage-shortage-related price hikes and a chart-position slide from 5th to as low as 14th place on Steam's bestseller rankings.

themes.theme: Sales collapse after the May price hike

insights.claim: The Steam Deck's chart position on Steam's revenue-based bestseller list fell from 5th place immediately after orders resumed in late May to as low as 14th place for two weeks in early July, before recovering slightly to 12th place currently.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable ranking signal showing a hardware product's demand deterioration in near-real time.
insights.locator: paragraph on chart position data from Boiling Steam

insights.claim: Throughout 2025 the Steam Deck never dropped below 7th place on the bestseller charts and was only rarely outside the Top 5, until Valve warned of 'intermittent shortages' starting in February 2026 and the device became completely unavailable until May.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Establishes the historical baseline against which the current sales decline is measured, making the drop starker.

insights.claim: Boiling Steam's back-of-the-envelope model estimates Steam Deck sales have fallen roughly 80 percent, from a 2025 baseline of about 11,000-18,000 units per week to a current estimate of just 1,400-3,000 units per week.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Gives investors/analysts a quantified (if rough) magnitude for the sales decline, useful for assessing Valve's hardware revenue trajectory.

insights.claim: The Steam Deck sold an estimated 4 million units in its first three years since its 2022 launch, making the current estimated weekly sales pace of 1,400-3,000 units a dramatic slowdown relative to its historical run rate.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Contextualizes the magnitude of the current slump against total lifetime unit volume.

insights.claim: Boiling Steam attributes the sales dip to a price increase that 'completely shut down demand for the Steam Deck,' with prices rising from a historical $400-$649 range to a current $789-$949 range.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: A direct causal claim linking a roughly 50-90% price increase to an ~80% estimated unit sales decline — a strong real-world data point on price elasticity for gaming hardware.
insights.quote: completely shut down demand for the Steam Deck.

insights.claim: At the new $789-$949 price range, consumers might start considering more powerful competing handheld gaming PCs in the same price bracket, such as ROG's Ally, Lenovo's Legion Go, or MSI's Claw.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Identifies specific named competitors that stand to gain share if Valve's pricing pushes the Steam Deck out of its prior value-leadership position.

themes.theme: Supply constraints as a contributing factor

insights.claim: The relative sales dip could also reflect ongoing Steam Deck supply shortages that Valve still describes as impacting availability 'intermittently' in some regions, driven by RAM and storage component shortages.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Suggests the sales decline may be partly supply-constrained rather than purely demand-driven, complicating attribution of the ~80% drop.

insights.claim: The Steam Deck was briefly unavailable again in North America shortly after relaunching at the new higher prices, indicating Valve still cannot always satisfy current market demand even with fewer buyers than before.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: A signal that component supply remains a binding constraint on Valve's hardware business independent of price-driven demand softness.

themes.theme: Implications for a Steam Deck successor

insights.claim: The author argues that in normal times a sales slowdown might signal Valve should move forward on a Steam Deck 2, but given current component pricing trends, a more powerful successor could require an even higher price to remain profitable.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Flags a strategic bind for Valve: the component cost pressures depressing current Deck sales would likely make a next-gen device even less affordably priced.
insights.quote: we shudder to think what Valve would have to charge to make a profit on a more powerful handheld today.
insights.locator: closing paragraph on Steam Deck 2 prospects
